mn122:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Sakyans at Kapilavatthu, in the Banyan Park.
mn122:10.1: Therefore, Ānanda, that *bhikkhu* should steady his mind [*cittaṁ*] inwardly alone, settle it, make it unified [*ekodiṁ*], and place it in *samādhi* in that same earlier *nimitta* of *samādhi* [*samādhinimitte*].
mn122:10.10: As he attends to the imperturbable, his mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into the imperturbable, becomes serene, becomes steady, becomes liberated.
mn122:10.11: When that is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:10.12: ‘As I attend to the imperturbable, my mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into the imperturbable, becomes serene, becomes steady, becomes liberated.’
mn122:10.13: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:10.3: As he attends to inner emptiness, his mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into inner emptiness, becomes serene, becomes steady, becomes liberated.
mn122:10.4: When that is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:10.5: ‘As I attend to inner emptiness, my mind [*cittaṁ*] enters into inner emptiness, becomes serene, becomes steady, becomes liberated.’
mn122:10.6: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:10.8: he attends to inner and outer emptiness …
mn122:11.1: If, Ānanda, while dwelling in this abiding, that *bhikkhu’s* mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to walking, he walks:
mn122:11.10: If, Ānanda, while dwelling in this abiding, that *bhikkhu’s* mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to lying down, he lies down:
mn122:11.11: ‘As I lie down in this way, bad, unwholesome *dhammas* of covetousness and distress will not overwhelm me.’
mn122:11.12: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:11.2: ‘As I walk in this way, bad, unwholesome *dhammas* of covetousness and distress will not overwhelm me.’
mn122:11.3: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:11.4: If, Ānanda, while dwelling in this abiding, that *bhikkhu’s* mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to standing, he stands:
mn122:11.5: ‘As I stand in this way, bad, unwholesome *dhammas* of covetousness and distress will not overwhelm me.’
mn122:11.6: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:11.7: If, Ānanda, while dwelling in this abiding, that *bhikkhu’s* mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to sitting, he sits:
mn122:11.8: ‘As I sit in this way, bad, unwholesome *dhammas* of covetousness and distress will not overwhelm me.’
mn122:11.9: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:12.1: If, Ānanda, while dwelling in this abiding, that *bhikkhu’s* mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to talk, then he reflects:
mn122:12.2: ‘This kind of talk is low, vulgar, ordinary, ignoble, unbeneficial, not leading to disillusionment, not to dispassion, not to cessation, not to stilling, not to direct knowledge, not to awakening, not to nibbāna; that is: talk about kings, talk about thieves, talk about ministers, talk about armies, talk about dangers, talk about wars, talk about food, talk about drinks, talk about clothes, talk about beds, talk about garlands, talk about scents, talk about relatives, talk about vehicles, talk about villages, talk about towns, talk about cities, talk about countries, talk about women, talk about liquor, street talk, well-side talk, talk about the dead, various kinds of talk, tales of the world, tales of the sea, and talk of “it is so” and “it is not so,” of being and not being. I will not speak such talk.’
mn122:12.3: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:12.4: And this kind of talk, Ānanda, is conducive to wearing away, suitable for clearing the mind’s hindrances, leading to complete disillusionment, to dispassion, to cessation, to stilling, to direct knowledge, to awakening, to nibbāna; that is: talk on fewness of wishes, talk on contentment, talk on seclusion [*pavivekakathā*], talk on non-entanglement, talk on arousing energy, talk on ethics, talk on *samādhi*, talk on discernment [*paññākathā*], talk on liberation, talk on the knowledge and vision of liberation. ‘I will speak such talk.’
mn122:12.5: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:13.1: If, Ānanda, while dwelling in this abiding, that *bhikkhu’s* mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to thought [*vitakkāya*], then he reflects:
mn122:13.2: ‘Those thoughts [*vitakkā*] that are low, vulgar, ordinary, ignoble, unbeneficial, not leading to disillusionment, not to dispassion, not to cessation, not to stilling, not to direct knowledge, not to awakening, not to nibbāna; that is: thought of sensuality [*kāmavitakko*], thought of ill will [*byāpādavitakko*], thought of harming [*vihiṁsāvitakko*]—I will not think such thoughts [*vitakke*].’
mn122:13.3: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:13.4: ‘And these thoughts [*vitakkā*], Ānanda, are noble, emancipating, and lead the one who practices accordingly to the complete ending of dukkha; that is: thought of renunciation [*nekkhammavitakko*], thought of non-ill will [*abyāpādavitakko*], thought of non-harming [*avihiṁsāvitakko*]. I will think such thoughts [*vitakke*].’
mn122:13.5: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:14.1: “These, Ānanda, are the five strings of sensual pleasure.
mn122:14.3: Forms cognizable by the eye, wished for, desirable, agreeable, likable, connected with sensuality, enticing;
mn122:14.7: tangible things cognizable by the body, wished for, desirable, agreeable, likable, connected with sensuality, enticing—
mn122:14.8: these, Ānanda, are the five strings of sensual pleasure.
mn122:15.1: In regard to these, a *bhikkhu* should repeatedly review his own mind [*cittaṁ*]:
mn122:15.10: then, when that is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:15.11: ‘The desire and passion for these five strings of sensual pleasure has been abandoned in me.’
mn122:15.12: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:15.2: ‘Is there, in me, with regard to these five strings of sensual pleasure, in one or another dimension [*āyatane*], an involvement of mind?’
mn122:15.3: If, Ānanda, while reviewing, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:15.4: ‘There is, in me, with regard to these five strings of sensual pleasure, in one or another dimension [*āyatane*], an involvement of mind,’
mn122:15.5: then, when that is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:15.6: ‘The desire and passion for these five strings of sensual pleasure has not been abandoned in me.’
mn122:15.7: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:15.8: But if, Ānanda, while reviewing, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:15.9: ‘There is not, in me, with regard to these five strings of sensual pleasure, in one or another dimension [*āyatane*], an involvement of mind,’
mn122:16.1: These, Ānanda, are the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhā*], in regard to which a *bhikkhu* should dwell contemplating rise and fall:
mn122:16.2: ‘Such is form, such is the arising of form, such is the passing away of form;
mn122:16.6: such is consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*], such is the arising of consciousness [*viññāṇassa*], such is the passing away of consciousness [*viññāṇassa*].’
mn122:17.1: As he dwells contemplating rise and fall in these five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*], whatever “I am” conceit there is with regard to the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*] is abandoned.
mn122:17.2: When that is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:17.3: ‘Whatever “I am” conceit there was in me with regard to these five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*], that has been abandoned.’
mn122:17.4: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:18.1: These, Ānanda, are *dhammas* that are exclusively wholesome, leading to the wholesome, noble, beyond the world, and not entered by the Evil One.
mn122:19.2: Seeing what benefit is a disciple fit to follow the teacher even while being sent away?”
mn122:19.3: “Our *dhammas*, bhante, are rooted in the *Bhagavā*, guided by the *Bhagavā*, with the *Bhagavā* as refuge. It would be good, bhante, if the meaning of this statement would become clear from the *Bhagavā* himself. Having heard it from the *Bhagavā*, the *bhikkhus* will remember it.”
mn122:2.1: Then, in the morning, the *Bhagavā* dressed, took his bowl and robe, and entered Kapilavatthu for alms.
mn122:2.11: “Ānanda, indeed many lodgings have been prepared in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan.
mn122:2.13: “Indeed, bhante, many lodgings have been prepared in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan.
mn122:2.15: It is the time for our robe-making, bhante.”
mn122:2.2: After walking for alms in Kapilavatthu, when he had returned from the almsround after the meal, he went to the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan for the daytime abiding.
mn122:2.3: Now at that time many lodgings had been prepared in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan.
mn122:2.4: The *Bhagavā* saw that many lodgings had been prepared in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan.
mn122:2.5: Seeing this, it occurred to the *Bhagavā*:
mn122:2.6: “Indeed, many lodgings have been prepared in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan.
mn122:2.7: Could it be that many *bhikkhus* are dwelling here?”
mn122:2.8: Now at that time Venerable Ānanda was doing robe-work together with many *bhikkhus* in the dwelling of Ghaṭa the Sakyan.
mn122:2.9: Then, in the evening, the *Bhagavā*, having risen from seclusion, went to the dwelling of Ghaṭa the Sakyan. Having gone there, he sat down on the seat prepared.
mn122:20.1: “Ānanda, it is not for the sake of discourses, chants, or expositions that a disciple is fit to follow the teacher, even while being sent away.
mn122:20.3: For, Ānanda, for a long time you have heard the *dhammas*, retained them, recited them verbally, examined them with the mind, and well penetrated them by view.
mn122:20.4: But this kind of talk, Ānanda, is conducive to wearing away, suitable for clearing the mind’s hindrances, leading to complete disillusionment, to dispassion, to cessation, to stilling, to direct knowledge, to awakening, to nibbāna; that is: talk on fewness of wishes, talk on contentment, talk on seclusion [*pavivekakathā*], talk on non-entanglement, talk on arousing energy, talk on ethics, talk on *samādhi*, talk on discernment [*paññākathā*], talk on liberation, talk on the knowledge and vision of liberation—for the sake of such talk, Ānanda, a disciple is fit to follow the teacher even while being sent away.
mn122:21.1: When this is so, Ānanda, there is danger involving a teacher; when this is so, there is danger involving a pupil; when this is so, there is danger involving one living the holy life.
mn122:22.1: And how, Ānanda, is there danger involving a teacher?
mn122:22.2: Here, Ānanda, some teacher resorts to a secluded lodging: a forest, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a jungle grove, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn122:22.3: As he dwells thus withdrawn, brahmins and householders, townspeople and country people keep coming after him.
mn122:22.4: As brahmins and householders, townspeople and country people keep coming after him, he comes to relish it, falls into greed, and turns back to abundance.
mn122:22.5: This, Ānanda, is called danger involving a teacher.
mn122:22.6: By danger involving a teacher, bad, unwholesome *dhammas* have slain him—defiling, leading to renewed existence, troublesome, ripening in dukkha, resulting in future birth, old age, and death.
mn122:22.7: That is how there is danger involving a teacher, Ānanda.
mn122:23.1: And how, Ānanda, is there danger involving a pupil?
mn122:23.2: Here again, Ānanda, a disciple of that teacher, nurturing that teacher’s seclusion [*vivekamanubrūhayamāno*],
mn122:23.3: resorts to a secluded lodging: a forest, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a jungle grove, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn122:23.4: As he dwells thus withdrawn, brahmins and householders, townspeople and country people keep coming after him.
mn122:23.5: As brahmins and householders, townspeople and country people keep coming after him, he comes to relish it, falls into greed, and turns back to abundance.
mn122:23.6: This, Ānanda, is called danger involving a pupil.
mn122:23.7: By danger involving a pupil, bad, unwholesome *dhammas* have slain him—defiling, leading to renewed existence, troublesome, ripening in dukkha, resulting in future birth, old age, and death.
mn122:23.8: That is how there is danger involving a pupil, Ānanda.
mn122:24.1: And how, Ānanda, is there danger involving one living the holy life?
mn122:24.10: This, Ānanda, is called danger involving one living the holy life.
mn122:24.11: By danger involving one living the holy life, bad, unwholesome *dhammas* have slain him—defiling, leading to renewed existence, troublesome, ripening in dukkha, resulting in future birth, old age, and death.
mn122:24.12: That is how there is danger involving one living the holy life, Ānanda.
mn122:24.13: And among these, Ānanda, the danger involving a teacher and the danger involving a pupil—this danger involving one living the holy life has a more painful result and a harsher result, and it also leads to downfall.
mn122:24.2: Here, Ānanda, a Tathāgata arises in the world, an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened one, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, fortunate, knower of the world, unsurpassed trainer of persons to be tamed, teacher of *devatās* and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.
mn122:24.3: He resorts to a secluded lodging: a forest, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a jungle grove, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn122:24.4: As he dwells thus withdrawn, brahmins and householders, townspeople and country people keep coming after him.
mn122:24.5: As brahmins and householders, townspeople and country people keep coming after him, he does not come to relish it, does not fall into greed, does not turn back to abundance.
mn122:24.6: But then, Ānanda, a disciple of that teacher, nurturing that teacher’s seclusion [*vivekamanubrūhayamāno*],
mn122:24.7: resorts to a secluded lodging: a forest, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a jungle grove, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn122:24.8: As he dwells thus withdrawn, brahmins and householders, townspeople and country people keep coming after him.
mn122:24.9: As brahmins and householders, townspeople and country people keep coming after him, he comes to relish it, falls into greed, and turns back to abundance.
mn122:25.1: Therefore, Ānanda, behave toward me as friends, not as enemies.
mn122:25.2: That will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn122:25.3: And how, Ānanda, do disciples behave toward the teacher as enemies, not as friends?
mn122:25.4: Here, Ānanda, the teacher teaches the *dhamma* to disciples, compassionate, seeking their welfare, out of compassion:
mn122:25.5: ‘This is for your welfare, this is for your *sukha*.’
mn122:25.6: But his disciples do not want to listen, do not lend ear, do not apply their minds to understanding, and they conduct themselves by straying from the teacher’s instruction.
mn122:25.7: That is how, Ānanda, disciples behave toward the teacher as enemies, not as friends.
mn122:26.1: And how, Ānanda, do disciples behave toward the teacher as friends, not as enemies?
mn122:26.2: Here, Ānanda, the teacher teaches the *dhamma* to disciples, compassionate, seeking their welfare, out of compassion:
mn122:26.3: ‘This is for your welfare, this is for your *sukha*.’
mn122:26.4: And his disciples want to listen, lend ear, apply their minds to understanding, and do not conduct themselves by straying from the teacher’s instruction.
mn122:26.5: That is how, Ānanda, disciples behave toward the teacher as friends, not as enemies.
mn122:26.6: Therefore, Ānanda, behave toward me as friends, not as enemies.
mn122:26.7: That will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn122:27.1: I will not work on you, Ānanda, the way a potter works on wet clay when it is still wet.
mn122:27.2: Reproving again and again, Ānanda, I will speak;
mn122:27.3: exhorting again and again, Ānanda, I will speak.
mn122:27.6: Satisfied, Venerable Ānanda rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn122:27.7: The Greater Emptiness is finished, the second.
mn122:3.1: “Ānanda, it is not fitting for a *bhikkhu* to delight in company, to enjoy company, to be devoted to delighting in company, to delight in groups, to enjoy groups, to be pleased with groups.
mn122:3.2: That a *bhikkhu* who delights in company, enjoys company, is devoted to delighting in company, delights in groups, enjoys groups, and is pleased with groups should obtain at will, without difficulty, without trouble, that pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion [*pavivekasukhaṁ*], the pleasure of stilling, the pleasure of awakening—this is not possible.
mn122:3.3: But that *bhikkhu* who dwells alone, withdrawn from the group—for that *bhikkhu* it may be expected that he will obtain at will, without difficulty, without trouble, that pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion [*pavivekasukhaṁ*], the pleasure of stilling, the pleasure of awakening—this is possible.
mn122:4.1: That a *bhikkhu* who delights in company, enjoys company, is devoted to delighting in company, delights in groups, enjoys groups, and is pleased with groups should dwell having entered either a temporary and delightful liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*], or one that is untemporary and unshakable—this is not possible.
mn122:4.2: But that *bhikkhu* who dwells alone, withdrawn from the group—for that *bhikkhu* it may be expected that he will dwell having entered either a temporary and delightful liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*], or one that is untemporary and unshakable—this is possible.
mn122:5.1: Ānanda, I do not see even a single form such that, for one infatuated with it and delighting in it, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, distress, and despair would not arise from the change and alteration of that form.
mn122:6.1: But this is a dwelling fully awakened to by the Tathāgata, namely:
mn122:6.2: through non-attention to all *nimittas*, to dwell having entered inner emptiness.
mn122:6.3: And if, Ānanda, while the Tathāgata is dwelling in this abiding, there come to him *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhunis*, laymen, laywomen, kings, royal ministers, wanderers of other sects, or disciples of wanderers of other sects,
mn122:6.4: then, Ānanda, with a mind [*cittena*] sloping to seclusion [*vivekaninnena*], inclining to seclusion [*vivekapoṇena*], tending to seclusion [*vivekapabbhārena*], withdrawn, delighting in renunciation, and finished with all *dhammas* that are a basis for the *āsavas*, he would speak only a talk connected with sending them away.
mn122:7.2: ‘May I dwell having entered inner emptiness,’ then, Ānanda, that *bhikkhu* should steady his mind [*cittaṁ*] inwardly alone, settle it, make it unified [*ekodiṁ*], and place it in *samādhi*.
mn122:7.3: And how, Ānanda, does a *bhikkhu* steady his mind [*cittaṁ*] inwardly alone, settle it, make it unified [*ekodiṁ*], and place it in *samādhi*?
mn122:8.1: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas* … enters and dwells in the first *jhāna* …
mn122:8.4: he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn122:8.5: In this way, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* steadies his mind [*cittaṁ*] inwardly alone, settles it, makes it unified [*ekodiṁ*], and places it in *samādhi*.
mn122:9.10: When that is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:9.11: ‘As I attend to the imperturbable, my mind [*cittaṁ*] does not enter into the imperturbable, does not become serene, does not become steady, does not become liberated.’
mn122:9.12: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:9.2: As he attends to inner emptiness, his mind [*cittaṁ*] does not enter into inner emptiness, does not become serene, does not become steady, does not become liberated.
mn122:9.3: When that is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:9.4: ‘As I attend to inner emptiness, my mind [*cittaṁ*] does not enter into inner emptiness, does not become serene, does not become steady, does not become liberated.’
mn122:9.5: Thus he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] of that.
mn122:9.7: he attends to inner and outer emptiness …
mn122:9.9: As he attends to the imperturbable, his mind [*cittaṁ*] does not enter into the imperturbable, does not become serene, does not become steady, does not become liberated.
